5: ton hyperlian apostolon (tōn hyperlian apostolōn, 'the super-apostles'): Paul's ironic designation for the itinerant missionaries who had infiltrated the Corinthian church claiming superior status (visions, letters of recommendation, impressive personal appearance, powerful rhetoric). The term hyperlian ('exceedingly great') is Paul's sneer-construction: these self-commending apostoloi compete on the Greco-Roman honorific terms Paul refuses to use. His 'fool's speech' (11:16-12:10) deliberately mimics their boasting — then inverts it by boasting only in weakness, suffering, and the thorn in the flesh.
1: Bear with me (anechesthe) in a little foolishness (aphrosunē) — the fool's speech (aphrosunē Logos) is a recognized rhetorical genre in Hellenistic literature; Paul adopts it ironically. The present imperative anechesthe (keep on bearing with me) signals that his self-defense has been ongoing and he needs their continued patience.
2: I feel a divine jealousy (zēlos theou) for you — zēlos can mean both jealousy and zeal; Paul uses the divine genitive to distinguish this from petty jealousy. He presented (hērmosamen, betrothed, from harmozō, to fit together) you as a pure virgin (parthenon hagnēn) to Christ — the betrothal/marriage metaphor frames the relationship between Christ and the church.
3: But I am afraid that as the serpent deceived Eve by his cunning (panourgia) — panourgia (craftiness, cunning) was a strongly negative term in Greek ethics; the Stoics identified it with the vice opposed to practical wisdom. Your thoughts (noēmata, minds, understanding) may be led astray from a sincere (haplotēs, simplicity, singlemindedness) and pure devotion to Christ.
4: If someone comes and proclaims another Jesus (allon Iesoun) — the triple another (allos, another; heteron, different; allos again) marks a qualitative difference from the gospel Paul preached. Heteron pneuma (a different Spirit) and heteron euangelion (a different gospel) echo Galatians 1:6-7's warning about perverting the gospel.
6: Even if I am unskilled (idiōtēs) in speaking — the idiōtēs (layperson, amateur, one without professional training) was the opposite of the trained rhetor in Greco-Roman culture. Paul freely concedes the accusation; but knowledge (gnōsis) is the substance of communication, not stylistic polish.
7: Or did I commit a sin (hamartian epoiēsa) in humbling myself so that you might be exalted, because I preached God's gospel to you free of charge? — the rhetorical question assumes the answer no. The humiliation/exaltation polarity echoes the Christ-hymn of Philippians 2:6-11: he humbled himself, therefore God exalted him.
8: I robbed (esylēsa) other churches by accepting support from them in order to serve you — sylao (to rob, plunder) is a strong metaphor; Paul uses the language of forced exaction to describe what was actually voluntary giving by the Macedonians, dramatizing the moral asymmetry between the churches that supported him and the Corinthians who received his service free.
9: I was not a burden to anyone — katanarkaō (to be burdensome, literally to numb, make torpid) is unusual; the brothers from Macedonia supplied (prosanaplēroō, filled up alongside) what I lacked. Paul deliberately avoided creating the patron-client dependency that would compromise his message.
10: As the truth of Christ (alētheia Christou) is in me — the oath formula using Christ as the medium of truth-telling; Paul's boasting (kauchēma) about this will not be silenced (phragēsetai) in the regions of Achaia.
11: And why? Because I do not love you? God knows I do! — the rhetorical question and immediate divine-appeal answer is a diatribe technique. The appeal to divine knowledge (ho theos oiden) functions as an oath warranty.
12: What I am doing I will continue to do, in order to undercut (ekkopsō, cut off, eliminate) those who want an opportunity to be regarded as our equals — Paul's continuing practice of free preaching removes the basis on which the intruders could claim to operate on the same level as Paul.
13: For such men are false apostles (pseudapostoloi), deceitful workers (ergatai dolioi) — the compound pseudapostoloi (false apostles) appears only here in the NT; it parallels pseudoprophētai (false prophets) and pseudodidaskaloi (false teachers). Disguising (metaschēmatizomenoi, transforming the shape of) themselves as apostles of Christ.
14: And no wonder, for Satan himself disguises himself (metaschēmatizetai) as an angel of light (angelos phōtos) — the present middle of metaschēmatizō (to change the outward form); the verbal repetition from v.13 identifies the intruders' disguise as satanic mimicry. Angel of light is a dramatic contrast with Satan's true nature.
15: So it is no surprise if his servants also disguise themselves as servants of righteousness (dikaiosynēs) — the servants (diakonoi) of righteousness is their self-presentation; Paul has already identified himself and his co-workers as servants/diakonoi in multiple senses (3:6; 6:4; 11:23). Their end (to telos) will correspond to their works — eschatological justice.
16: Let no one think me foolish (aphrona); but even if you do, accept me as a fool, so that I too may boast a little — the Stoic sage could not be a fool; the Cynic philosopher embraced the role of the social outsider-critic. Paul appropriates both as ironic self-positioning.
17: What I am saying with this boastful confidence (hypostasei) I say not as the Lord would but as a fool — hypostasis (standing, substance, confidence) — Paul uses this word for the foundations of faith in Hebrews 11:1. Here it describes the boastful confidence he is about to display, which he explicitly marks as not in the Lord's pattern.
18: Since many boast according to the flesh (kata sarka), I too will boast — the boasting kata sarka (according to worldly standards) is precisely what Paul argued against in 1 Corinthians 1-3. He enters the game to show that he beats the intruders at their own game, then exposes the game itself as meaningless.
19: For you gladly bear with (anechesthe) fools, being wise (phronimoi) yourselves! — the irony: the Corinthians pride themselves on wisdom (sophia) but are exposed as those who bear with fools — which makes them fools themselves (Prov 26:4-5: answer a fool according to his folly).
20: For you bear it if someone enslaves you (katadouloī), devours you (katesthiō), takes advantage of you (lambanei), puts on airs (epairetai), or strikes you in the face — the five verbs of exploitation culminate in the physical: strikes you in the face (ei tis hymas eis prosōpon derei). The progression from economic exploitation to physical violence maps the anatomy of false apostolic authority.
21: To my shame, I must say, we were too weak for that! — the cutting irony: Paul confesses his shameful weakness was his refusal to exploit the congregation. Whatever anyone else dares to boast of — I am speaking as a fool — I also dare to boast.
22: Are they Hebrews (Hebraioi)? So am I. Are they Israelites (Israelitai)? So am I. Are they offspring of Abraham (sperma Abraam)? So am I — the three terms represent ethnic identity (Hebrews = Hebrew-speaking Jews), covenant membership (Israelites = belonging to Israel), and messianic lineage (seed of Abraham = heirs of the promise). Paul matches each credential.
23: Are they servants (diakonoi) of Christ? I am a better one — I am talking like a madman (paraphronōn lalō) — the parenthetical disclaimer intensifies before the peristasis catalogue begins. The suffering catalogue (peristasis catalogue) that follows was a recognized form in Hellenistic moral philosophy for demonstrating the true sage's virtue through endurance.
24: Five times I received at the hands of the Jews the forty lashes less one — the synagogue discipline of thirty-nine stripes (Deut 25:2-3 capped at forty; practice reduced to thirty-nine to avoid accidental exceeding). This is not recorded in Acts; Paul's letters preserve historical data Acts omits.
25: Three times I was beaten with rods (rhabdois), once I was stoned (elithasthēn), three times I was shipwrecked (enauagēsa) — the rhabdos-beating was Roman civic punishment (Acts 16:22 at Philippi). The stoning is the Lystra incident (Acts 14:19). The three shipwrecks pre-date the Acts 27 shipwreck that Luke describes.
26: On frequent journeys, in danger (kindynois) from rivers, danger from robbers — the eight-fold danger list (rivers, robbers, my own people, Gentiles, city, wilderness, sea, false brothers) is structured as a comprehensive catalogue of every threat environment.
27: In toil and hardship, through many a sleepless night, in hunger and thirst, often without food, in cold and exposure (gymnōtēti, nakedness) — the catalogue shifts from external dangers to physical privations. Gymnōtēs (nakedness, inadequate clothing) reflects actual material destitution, not metaphor.
28: And, apart from other things, there is the daily pressure on me of my anxiety (merimna) for all the churches — merimna (anxious concern, care) is the word Jesus used for the anxious care about daily necessities (Matt 6:25-34). Paul redirects it: his anxiety is pastoral, not material.
29: Who is weak (asthenei), and I am not weak? Who is made to fall (skandalizetai), and I am not indignant (ouk pyroō, not on fire)? — the two rhetorical questions describe Paul's pastoral identification with the congregation's vulnerabilities. Pyroō (to burn, be inflamed) is used for passion and indignation.
30: If I must boast, I will boast of the things that show my weakness (astheneia) — the inversion of the boast-genre: the conventional peristasis catalogue demonstrated strength through endurance; Paul's catalogue concludes by claiming weakness as the proper content of apostolic boasting.
31: The God and Father of the Lord Jesus, he who is blessed forever, knows that I am not lying — a solemn oath formula at the transition from the general catalogue to the specific Damascus escape incident. The divine knowledge (oiden) serves as the ultimate truth-warrant.
32: At Damascus, the governor (ho ethnarchēs) under King Aretas guarded (ephroureito) the city of Damascus in order to seize (piasai) me — ethnarchēs was an administrative title for the governor of an ethnic community within a larger political structure. The historical detail is corroborated by Nabataean royal history.
33: I was let down (echalasthēn, was lowered) in a basket (en sargani, in a woven basket) through a window in the wall and escaped (exephygon, fled out from) his hands — the aorist passives describe the events from Paul's perspective as things done to/for him. The basket (sargane) was a large wicker or rope basket used for cargo.
